summary: Solid TDD work with good test coverage for happy paths and error cases. A few gaps: listTokens error path loses error messages unlike its siblings, error-path response.json() calls can throw on non-JSON bodies (untested), a type assertion in parseErrorMessage, and listTokens network errors are untested.

suggestions.description: response.json() on error path can throw if server returns non-JSON body (e.g. HTML 502 from proxy)
suggestions.rationale: createToken and revokeToken both call response.json() inside the \!response.ok branch. If the error response is not valid JSON, this throws and falls into the catch block, which works but gives a misleading json parse error message instead of the HTTP status. More importantly, no test exercises this path.
suggestions.suggestion: Either wrap the error-path response.json() in its own try/catch with a fallback message like "Request failed with status {status}", or add tests that verify behavior when the error response is not JSON.

suggestions.description: listTokens error path discards error message unlike createToken and revokeToken
suggestions.rationale: createToken (line 78) and revokeToken (line 127) both parse and return the error message from the response body. listTokens (line 99) silently returns {success: false, tokens: []} with no error field. The test asserts this behavior, but the inconsistency means callers of listTokens cannot tell the user WHY the request failed. The test encodes this gap as if it were intentional.
suggestions.suggestion: Have listTokens parse the error body and include an error field in the failure result, consistent with the other two functions. Update the test accordingly.

suggestions.description: listTokens has no network error test
suggestions.rationale: createToken and revokeToken both have explicit network error tests (mockRejectedValueOnce). listTokens has none. The implementation does handle it (catch block at line 114), but there is no test proving it. Untested code is unverified code.
suggestions.suggestion: Add a test: listTokens returns {success: false, tokens: []} when fetch rejects with a network error.

suggestions.description: Type assertion: data as Record<string, unknown> in parseErrorMessage
suggestions.rationale: The CLAUDE.md guidelines say no type assertions. This is a minor one since it is guarded by the typeof/null/in checks above it, but it is still an as-cast that could be avoided.
suggestions.suggestion: Use a type guard function or narrow without casting. For example: function isErrorBody(data: unknown): data is {error: string} { return typeof data === "object" && data \!== null && "error" in data && typeof (data as any).error === "string"; } — or simply inline the narrowing without casting.

suggestions.description: listTokens GET assertion uses toBeUndefined() for method instead of asserting GET explicitly
suggestions.rationale: The test asserts options.method is undefined to verify a GET request. This is fragile — it relies on the implementation detail that omitting method defaults to GET. If someone later explicitly sets method: "GET", this test breaks despite correct behavior.
suggestions.suggestion: Either assert options.method is undefined OR "GET" (e.g. expect(options.method ?? "GET").toBe("GET")), or just remove the assertion since the URL and response parsing already verify the right endpoint is called.

suggestions.description: Defensive field-by-field type checking in listTokens parsing may be more than tests require
suggestions.rationale: Each field of each token entry is individually type-checked with typeof fallbacks to empty string. The tests only exercise well-formed data. If the API returns malformed tokens, the empty-string fallbacks silently produce invalid entries rather than filtering them out or erroring. No test exercises this defensive logic, so it is untested complexity.
suggestions.suggestion: Either add tests for malformed token entries (missing fields, wrong types) to justify this defensive parsing, or simplify by trusting the API shape and using a simpler cast — the defensive code should earn its keep through tests.
